feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

section_path: Creativity Works! Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Creativity Works! is Europe's leading coalition representing our vibrant cultural and creative sectors. Our geographically-diverse, creative ecosystem-spanning membership sets us apart, bringing together book publishers and booksellers; picture agencies; music publishers and independent music labels; producers, publishers and distributors of film and audiovisual content; cinema operators; commercial broadcasters and sports event organisers, as well as video game companies, all under one single European umbrella.

section_path: Creativity Works! Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Creativity Works! members create, produce, finance, publish, distribute and showcase creative content - be it films, TV series, shows, original content, news programmes, music, pictures, books, video games, sports events, and much more.

section_path: Creativity Works! Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Our industries not only enrich European society culturally but are also economic powerhouses at the forefront of creative, digital and technological innovation. The creative and cultural industries employ 17 million people, represent $6.9\%$ of Europe's GDP, and contribute €253 billion to the EU economy, surpassing sectors such as telecommunications and pharmaceuticals. Over $90\%$ of companies in our sectors are SMEs.

section_path: Creativity Works! Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The creative and cultural industries are vital to Europe's social and economic fabric fostering diversity and competitiveness whilst following high consumer protection standards. Culture, creativity and sports play an important role in connecting Europe's citizens across age groups. Our industries enrich the lives of all generations while providing meaningful employment and opportunities for young Europeans to express themselves, develop skills, and contribute to Europe's evolving cultural narrative while promoting an inspiring lifestyle.

section_path: Creativity Works! Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We welcome the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's impact assessment for the Digital Fairness Act and would like to highlight the following:

Today, Europeans benefit from more than 150 million musical tracks, on several thousand licensed digital streaming services; over 3 million e-books and audiobooks in addition to a vast print book selection, available in European bookshops; a vast array of sports content; countless images; more than 12,000 audiovisual media services, counting linear TV channels as well as non-linear services such as SVOD, TVOD, AVOD and other forms of online video services, beyond the experience offered by over 10,000 European cinemas on more than 27,000 screens; and a vast array of video games enjoyed by over half of the EU's population.

This dynamic ecosystem is sustained by the ongoing investment and innovation of the cultural and creative industries in professionally created content. These sectors are committed to providing audiences with choice, with high quality content and upholding responsible business practices in line with EU consumer protection laws, including offering flexible subscription options, transparent cancellation processes, clear and transparent information to consumers, including information and recommendations about age appropriate content for audiovisual media services and video games.

Cultural and creative works are professionally produced and, importantly, editorially responsible and accountable, ensuring quality and control of the content offered to European audiences. The sectors operate within existing efficient regulatory frameworks, including EU consumer protection law and relevant sector-specific legislation. These regulatory frameworks are supported by self and co regulatory frameworks.

Our membership is concerned that any legislative changes could unintentionally disrupt existing business models, for instance, by enabling the abuse of refund mechanisms, allowing consumers to claim refunds after having fully accessed or enjoyed digital content. Such measures could undermine the ability to recover investments and weaken confidence in Europe's creative economy and its ability to produce top-tier cultural and creative works.

The downstream impact of any measures on the entire cultural and creative ecosystem should also be kept in mind. Changes to consumer rules do not only impact consumer-facing cultural and creative businesses but the entire ecosystem around those businesses e.g. authors, creators, actors, developers, producers etc.

It is therefore important to uphold commercial freedom and a principle-based consumer protection framework, to allow for flexibility for businesses in how they offer their content to European audiences in ways that fit their model and audience without limiting their choices and causing harm to them and businesses by limiting downstream investment impact. The existing legal framework retains enough flexibility to accommodate the diversity of business models across cultural and creative sectors while ensuring a high level of consumer protection.

section_path: 2. Differentiating digital environments, avoiding one-size-fits-all approach
paragraphs.text: We recognise that there may be issues in certain parts of the digital ecosystem but a "one-size-fits-all" approach would fail to differentiate between distinct digital environments. This is particularly important when dealing with a number of features mentioned in the consultation. On algorithm-driven, engagement-maximising platforms, where content is generated by users and ranked without editorial responsibility or liability, such features can contribute to problematic usage patterns, including compulsive scrolling.

section_path: 2. Differentiating digital environments, avoiding one-size-fits-all approach
paragraphs.text: These issues are well-documented in social media contexts, where recommender systems may prioritise extreme or emotive content to maximise engagement. By contrast, these features serve fundamentally different purposes in our curated environments.

section_path: 2. Differentiating digital environments, avoiding one-size-fits-all approach
paragraphs.text: Imposing restrictions on systems in curated creative and cultural environments would equate to limiting editorial discretion, undermining editorial independence, and undermining the competitiveness of our sectors. Editorially accountable providers already apply robust parental

section_path: 2. Differentiating digital environments, avoiding one-size-fits-all approach
paragraphs.text: controls, age-appropriate content curation and settings, ensuring user control over their viewing experience.

section_path: 3. Importance of enforcement and of maintaining principle-based horizontal EU consumer protection rules
paragraphs.text: Whilst at CW! we fully support the goal of protecting consumers from unfair digital practices; we believe the DSA and Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, but also the AVMSD, already comprehensively address many of the issues highlighted by the DFA consultation.

section_path: 3. Importance of enforcement and of maintaining principle-based horizontal EU consumer protection rules
paragraphs.text: At Creativity Works!, we recognise the importance of protecting consumers while nurturing creativity and attracting investments. An overhaul of the basis on which EU consumer legislation is founded on is, in our view, not necessary. We would recommend that the Commission focus on more consistent enforcement of existing and newly adopted laws. However in areas such as piracy, including live, where there are clear shortfalls in the enforceability of existing rules, we would recommend that the Commission takes a targeted, proportionate and evidence-based approach to addressing them.

section_path: 3. Importance of enforcement and of maintaining principle-based horizontal EU consumer protection rules
paragraphs.text: Online piracy is a consumer protection issue, as people who access pirated content are more likely to have their identity stolen and to be victims of fraud and scams. On top of that, pirate platforms do not offer child protection tools, potentially exposing children to pornography and other harmful content. Interpol and Europol studies have warned that criminal organisations are moving online, substantiating that "digital content piracy increasingly overlaps with cybercrime, as criminals use various technical means to breach both intellectual property and data security".

section_path: 4. Guiding principles on the Digital Fairness Act for the cultural and creative industries
paragraphs.text: In conclusion, we would recommend that the Commission prioritise enforcement of the laws in place, while carefully targeting any additional intervention to problematic online environments and to those with enforcement gaps. This approach would avoid imposing disproportionate burdens on sectors that already demonstrate high levels of consumer protection and regulatory compliance.

section_path: 4. Guiding principles on the Digital Fairness Act for the cultural and creative industries
paragraphs.text: At a time where Europe's competitiveness is at stake, we would respectfully caution against introducing additional requirements that could override existing provisions and create unnecessary administrative complexity for creative and cultural sectors, which create 15 million jobs and contribute 6.9% of the EU's GDP.

section_path: 4. Guiding principles on the Digital Fairness Act for the cultural and creative industries
paragraphs.text: Culture and sports are among Europe's biggest assets and it is essential that the European Union considers the role and value of the EU's cultural and creative industries, including sports.
